factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies market using ANZSIC C2221 or ANZSIC06 C2221 classification
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly defines the industry as “ANZSIC C2221 – Structural Steel Fabricating” in the Definitions section, thereby identifying the market using the required ANZSIC C2221/ANZSIC06 C2221 classification.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States employment figures in range 4,200-4,600 for multiple years within 2018-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response provides employment figures for multiple years within 2018–2024 that fall in the 4,200–4,600 range (e.g., 2020: ~4,600; 2021: ~4,500). These satisfy the criterion’s requirement for multiple years in-range within the specified period.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States market revenue approximately NZD 1.4-1.9 billion for 2023-2024 period
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states a 2024 market value of NZD 1.8 billion and indicates a trough around NZD 1.45 billion in 2021, which places the 2023–2024 period approximately within the NZD 1.4–1.9 billion range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States domestic fabrication capacity between 120,000-150,000 tonnes per annum
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states installed domestic fabrication capacity as “120,000–150,000 tonnes (2023–2024)” in the fabrication volumes/capacity section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Provides geographic locations for at least 3 major fabricators (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists five major fabricators but does not provide their geographic locations (e.g., Auckland, Christchurch, Whangārei, Lower Hutt, Timaru) for at least three of them. No city/region is given for Culham Engineering, D&H Steel, John Jones Steel, or Jensen Steel (only vague references like “Auckland/commercial market share”).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies HS codes 7207-7216 or relevant subset for structural steel imports
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies HS codes 7207–7216 as structural steel inputs and provides a table with selected tariff lines within that range (e.g., 7207, 7208, 7209, 7210, 7213, 7214, 7216). This satisfies the requirement to specify HS codes 7207–7216 or a relevant subset for structural steel imports.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States most structural steel inputs under HS 7207-7216 are duty-free under NZ Normal Tariff, while noting some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% rates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists multiple HS 7207–7216 lines as having a 5% “Normal Tariff Rate” and mentions FTAs making effective rates 0%, but it does not state that most structural steel inputs are duty-free under the NZ Normal Tariff. It also fails to note that only some tariff lines (e.g., certain 7216 sections) carry 5% while most are duty-free.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link steel demand: approximately 30,000-32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000-5,000 tonnes structural
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives City Rail Link steel demand as “~25,000 tonnes structural/reinforcing steel,” but it does not state approximately 30,000–32,000 tonnes reinforcing and/or 3,000–5,000 tonnes structural. The required tonnage ranges are not present anywhere in the CRL section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States City Rail Link completion or opening circa 2026
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that City Rail Link’s “operational opening” is “late 2025/early 2026,” which places completion/opening circa 2026.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Te Kaha/Christchurch Stadium roof steel approximately 9,000 tonnes
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Te Kaha (Christchurch Multipurpose Arena) steel demand as “~6,000 tonnes” and does not mention the roof steel being approximately 9,000 tonnes. The specific figure of ~9,000 tonnes for the stadium roof steel is absent.
The response includes a section on “Te Kaha (Christchurch Multipurpose Arena)” and explicitly states “Completion: To open 2026.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies New Dunedin Hospital as major infrastructure project requiring significant structural steel components
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions “Additional: Major Hospitals (Auckland, Dunedin)” and gives a generic steel-demand estimate, but it does not specifically identify the New Dunedin Hospital as a major infrastructure project nor state that it requires significant structural steel components.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Reconciles conflicting market size figures by explaining scope definition differences (narrow product vs. broad service boundaries)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not reconcile conflicting market size figures by contrasting a narrow product scope versus broader service boundaries. It provides single-point revenue/tonnage estimates and some definitions, but it never identifies two conflicting market-size numbers and then explains the discrepancy as a scope-definition issue.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes construction fabricators from distributors/mills/producers when identifying top companies (e.g., Steel & Tube, Pacific Steel)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes it excludes producers/mills (e.g., “NZ Steel is NOT included as it is a producer/mill”) and states the top-5 list is intended to be fabricators rather than distributors/mills. It also flags Steel & Tube as a supplier/fabricator with non-structural revenue, indicating an attempt to distinguish categories.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains why low/zero tariffs remove price umbrella, making margins dependent on global mill prices/FX/freight rather than border protection
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes that effective import tariffs are essentially 0% under FTAs, but it does not explain the economic mechanism that low/zero tariffs remove a “price umbrella” and therefore shift margin drivers to global mill prices, FX, and freight rather than border protection. It also does not connect tariff absence to competitive pricing pressure on domestic fabricators’ margins.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies actionable method to obtain class-level data (e.g., filter Stats NZ AES CSV by ANZSIC L4 code for C2221)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides an actionable replication method for class-level data by instructing the user to download Stats NZ industry tables and filter by ANZSIC code C2221 (Structural Steel Fabricating). This is a concrete, step-by-step approach analogous to filtering a Stats NZ CSV by an ANZSIC L4 code.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects employment or revenue trends to construction cycle timing (e.g., 2023 peak preceding mid-2022 slowdown)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly links employment and revenue changes to construction-cycle timing, noting a post-2019 peak, a 2020–2021 pandemic/project-delay downturn, and a late-2022 to 2024 soft period affecting employment. It frames these shifts as tracking construction cycles and infrastructure investment timing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Validates stated fabrication capacity/throughput by aggregating steel demand across multiple named projects with tonnages
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response lists several projects with estimated steel demand tonnages (e.g., CRL 25,000t; Transmission Gully 8,000–12,000t; Te Kaha 6,000t) but does not aggregate these named project tonnages to validate a stated fabrication capacity/throughput figure. It makes general statements about capacity exceeding demand without showing a summed calculation across projects.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies absent official tonnage series and proposes specific triangulation approach using SCNZ capacity, trade data, or project disclosures
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides tonnage and capacity figures and cites SCNZ industry updates, but it does not explicitly identify that official Stats NZ/MBIE tonnage series are absent. It also does not lay out a specific triangulation method combining SCNZ capacity with trade data or project disclosures to estimate fabricated tonnage.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Attributes fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection rather than global price/FX exposure
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not attribute fabricator margin pressure or competitive challenges to NZ import tariffs or border protection. It states that effective import tariffs are essentially 0% under FTAs and instead highlights exposure to global price volatility, freight delays, and FX-related supply chain risks.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents all quantitative data with explicit units (NZD, tonnes, %), specific timeframes (years/FY), and measurement precision
presentation-quality.explanation: Some quantitative data include units (e.g., NZD, tonnes, %, HS codes) and time references, but many figures lack explicit timeframes (e.g., “Market Value (2024)” vs FY), precision (wide ranges like “100,000–105,000 tonnes/year”), or consistent units (e.g., “$17.9m–$24.6m” without NZD). Not all quantitative claims are fully specified with explicit units, years/FY, and precision.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis into distinct sections addressing each requested dimension (market size, companies, tariffs, projects, risks)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized into numbered sections that separately cover market size/growth (Section 1), companies (Section 2), tariffs (Section 3), projects (Section 4), and risks/opportunities (Section 6). These sections align with each requested dimension.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional market research tone without promotional language, speculation, or unsupported forward claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response mostly uses a market research tone, but it includes speculative/unsupported forward-looking claims (e.g., “Peak project years (2024–26) may push demand…”, “operational opening late 2025/early 2026”, and “expected >30,000 tonnes… industry assumption”) and some promotional phrasing like “complete replicability” and “exhaustive source referencing.”

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents historical employment, revenue, or capacity data (2018-2024) in tables or structured year-by-year format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response provides historical employment and capacity/tonnage figures for multiple years between 2018 and 2024 as explicit year-by-year bullet lists (e.g., employment for 2018, 2020, 2021, 2023, 2024) and a stated 2018–2024 tonnage range, which is a structured year-by-year/historical format.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses colloquial phrasing, rhetorical questions, or informal register inappropriate for investment-grade research
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, report-style register throughout, using structured headings, tables, and neutral language. It does not include rhetorical questions or notably colloquial/informal phrasing inappropriate for investment-grade research.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses excessive wordiness, filler phrases, or unnecessary elaboration that extends length without adding substantive information
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is highly verbose, adding extensive methodology, replication instructions, extra sections (e.g., supply-demand reconciliation), and multiple additional projects beyond the requested three, which increases length without consistently adding necessary substance for the query.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Business Demography or equivalent Stats NZ publication for employment figures
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides employment figures but cites SCNZ and IBISWorld for those numbers and does not cite Stats NZ Business Demography (or another Stats NZ publication) as the source for employment. It mentions “Stats NZ/MBIE” in methodology but gives no Stats NZ Business Demography link, publication date, or explicit citation tied to the employment data.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites SCNZ, IBISWorld, MBIE, and NZ Customs sources but does not cite Stats NZ Annual Enterprise Survey (AES) or any equivalent Stats NZ economic survey for revenue/sales data. It mentions “Stats NZ/MBIE” generally for employment/business counts without linking to AES or providing AES-based revenue figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites NZ Customs Working Tariff Document or official NZ tariff schedule for HS code duty rates
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites the NZ Customs “Working Tariff Document” via a direct hyperlink and uses it as the official tariff schedule source for HS code duty rates.

citation-quality.requirement: References Steel Construction NZ (SCNZ) or industry body for fabrication capacity/throughput data
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Steel Construction New Zealand (SCNZ) “Structural Steel Industry Updates” as the source for tonnage, installed capacity, and spare capacity/throughput data, including multiple SCNZ PDF links.

citation-quality.requirement: Either references NZ Companies Office as a data source OR notes that private fabricators have limited public financials
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites the “Companies Register – New Zealand” and links to the Companies Office search portal as a data source for company information.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites specific data sources for at least 2 major infrastructure projects (CRL, Te Kaha, or hospital projects)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites specific sources for at least two major projects: City Rail Link (cityraillink.co.nz and NZ Steel news page) and Te Kaha (te-kaha.co.nz), and also provides sources for Transmission Gully (OAG report) and Waikato Green Steel (Beehive release).
